# Last Time

::: {.incremental}
+ **How the Compiler Works**
+ **The Symbol Table**
+ **Data Type Conversion and Casting**
+ **Signed vs Unsigned Variables**
+ **Overflow and Underflow**
+ **Fixed-Width Integers and size_t**
+ **Determining Variable Size**
:::

::: notes
Brief recall of lecture 02.01. Students should remember that C++ checks types at compile time, silently converts some values, and can behave badly at numeric boundaries. This lecture shifts from how code is processed to how a program communicates through the terminal.
:::

<!-- Slide 1 -->

# Today's Agenda

- `cout`
- `cin`
- Output Manipulators
- Terminal Color Codes
- Table Architectures

::: notes

:::

<!-- Slide 2 -->

{{< include topic_1_cout.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_2_cin.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_3_output_manipulators.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_4_terminal_color_codes.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_5_table_architectures.qmd >}}
{{< include topic_6_coding_exercise.qmd >}}

---

# Key Takeaways

- `cout` sends text, variable values, and line breaks to the terminal.
- `cin` reads typed input into variables and treats whitespace as a separator.
- Manipulators from `<iomanip>` control width, alignment, and decimal precision.
- ANSI color codes can emphasize terminal output when reset carefully.
- Tables are repeated output patterns designed for scanning.

<!-- Slide 65 -->

---

# Next Time

- Debugging workflow
- Reading compiler errors
- More careful input handling

<!-- Slide 66 -->

---

# Appendix

<!-- Slide 67 -->

---

## cout: Good Practices

- Put prompts before reads and labels before values.
- Use `"\n"` or `endl` intentionally; both create a new line, but `endl` also flushes.
- Keep chained output readable by breaking long statements across lines.

<!-- Slide 68 -->

---

## cout: Common Mistakes

- Putting variable names inside quotes and expecting their values to print.
- Forgetting spaces inside string literals, causing output to run together.
- Ending every prompt with `endl` when the input should appear on the same line.

<!-- Slide 69 -->

---

## cout: More Examples

```cpp
cout << "Total: $" << total << "\n";
cout << "Tax: $" << tax << "\n";
cout << "Grand total: $" << grandTotal << "\n";
```

<!-- Slide 70 -->

---

## cout: Practice Problems

1. Print your name and favorite number on separate lines.
2. Print a sentence that includes one `string` variable and one `int` variable.
3. Write a prompt that keeps the cursor on the same line.

<!-- Slide 71 -->

---

## cout: AI Search Terms

- "C++ cout insertion operator"
- "C++ endl vs newline"
- "C++ print variable with text"

<!-- Slide 72 -->

---

## cout: Questions for Reflection

- Why does output formatting matter even when the values are correct?
- When would a prompt on the same line be clearer than a prompt on its own line?

<!-- Slide 73 -->

---

## cin: Good Practices

- Prompt immediately before reading.
- Match the variable type to the kind of input expected.
- Keep early examples to single-word `string` input until full-line input is introduced.

<!-- Slide 74 -->

---

## cin: Common Mistakes

- Typing words when the program expects a number.
- Expecting `cin >> name` to read a full name with spaces.
- Reading values in a different order than the prompt asks for them.

<!-- Slide 75 -->

---

## cin: More Examples

```cpp
double length;
double width;

cout << "Length and width: ";
cin >> length >> width;

cout << "Area: " << length * width << endl;
```

<!-- Slide 76 -->

---

## cin: Practice Problems

1. Read a user's first name and age, then print both values.
2. Read two integers and print their sum.
3. Read a price and quantity, then print the subtotal.

<!-- Slide 77 -->

---

## cin: AI Search Terms

- "C++ cin extraction operator"
- "C++ cin whitespace behavior"
- "C++ read multiple values cin"

<!-- Slide 78 -->

---

## cin: Questions for Reflection

- Why should the prompt come before the read?
- Why does `cin` need to know the receiving variable's type?

<!-- Slide 79 -->

---

## Output Manipulators: Good Practices

- Include `<iomanip>` only when formatting tools are needed.
- Use `fixed << setprecision(2)` for money-like values.
- Choose column widths after testing with realistic values.

<!-- Slide 80 -->

---

## Output Manipulators: Common Mistakes

- Forgetting that `setw` applies only to the next item.
- Expecting `setprecision(2)` to mean two decimal places without `fixed`.
- Using column widths too small for the data.

<!-- Slide 81 -->

---

## Output Manipulators: More Examples

```cpp
cout << fixed << setprecision(1);
cout << left << setw(10) << "Day"
     << right << setw(8) << "Temp" << endl;
cout << left << setw(10) << "Monday"
     << right << setw(8) << 71.4 << endl;
```

<!-- Slide 82 -->

---

## Output Manipulators: Practice Problems

1. Print a three-column table with a name, score, and average.
2. Format a `double` so it always prints two decimal places.
3. Use `setw` to right-align three numbers.

<!-- Slide 83 -->

---

## Output Manipulators: AI Search Terms

- "C++ iomanip setw"
- "C++ fixed setprecision"
- "C++ left right output alignment"

<!-- Slide 84 -->

---

## Output Manipulators: Questions for Reflection

- Why do numbers usually read better right-aligned?
- Which formatting settings affect one item, and which stay active?

<!-- Slide 85 -->

---

## Terminal Color Codes: Good Practices

- Store escape codes as named `const string` values.
- Use color only when it communicates status or emphasis.
- Reset with `NORM` after every colored message.

<!-- Slide 86 -->

---

## Terminal Color Codes: Common Mistakes

- Forgetting to reset and accidentally coloring later output.
- Adding color to every line until nothing stands out.
- Using colors that do not display well on the target terminal.

<!-- Slide 87 -->

---

## Terminal Color Codes: More Examples

```cpp
const string BLUE = "\033[034m";
const string NORM = "\033[0m";

cout << BLUE << "Info: loading settings." << NORM << endl;
```

<!-- Slide 88 -->

---

## Terminal Color Codes: Practice Problems

1. Print one red error message and reset afterward.
2. Print a green success message after a calculation.
3. Add a blue heading to a formatted report.

<!-- Slide 89 -->

---

## Terminal Color Codes: AI Search Terms

- "C++ ANSI escape codes terminal color"
- "C++ color text console"
- "ANSI reset code terminal"

<!-- Slide 90 -->

---

## Terminal Color Codes: Questions for Reflection

- When does color improve output, and when does it become noise?
- Why is a reset code part of the output contract?

<!-- Slide 91 -->

---

## Table Architectures: Good Practices

- Decide column order before writing output statements.
- Use the same widths for the header and every row.
- Align text left and numbers right unless there is a clear reason not to.

<!-- Slide 92 -->

---

## Table Architectures: Common Mistakes

- Changing widths from one row to the next.
- Forgetting that long text can break a narrow column.
- Printing decimals with inconsistent precision.

<!-- Slide 93 -->

---

## Table Architectures: More Examples

```cpp
cout << left << setw(12) << "Course"
     << right << setw(8) << "Units" << endl;
cout << left << setw(12) << "CISP 360"
     << right << setw(8) << 4 << endl;
```

<!-- Slide 94 -->

---

## Table Architectures: Practice Problems

1. Print a two-row table of products and prices.
2. Add a separator line under the table header.
3. Format a small grade report with names and scores.

<!-- Slide 95 -->

---

## Table Architectures: AI Search Terms

- "C++ formatted table output setw"
- "C++ align columns cout"
- "C++ console receipt formatting"

<!-- Slide 96 -->

---

## Table Architectures: Questions for Reflection

- What makes a table easier to scan than a paragraph of values?
- How can a table structure reveal mistakes in output?

<!-- Slide 97 -->

---

## Coding Exercise 2-1: Good Practices

- Read all required values before calculating totals.
- Use clear variable names such as `quantity`, `price`, and `total`.
- Test with small numbers first so the expected result is easy to verify.

<!-- Slide 98 -->

---

## Coding Exercise 2-1: Common Mistakes

- Calculating the total before reading the input values.
- Forgetting `fixed << setprecision(2)` for receipt output.
- Coloring the success message without resetting afterward.

<!-- Slide 99 -->

---

## Coding Exercise 2-1: More Examples

```cpp
double total = quantity * price;

cout << fixed << setprecision(2);
cout << "Total: $" << total << endl;
```

<!-- Slide 100 -->

---

## Coding Exercise 2-1: Practice Problems

1. Add sales tax to the receipt.
2. Add a colored warning if quantity is zero.
3. Print the receipt title in blue.

<!-- Slide 101 -->

---

## Coding Exercise 2-1: AI Search Terms

- "C++ receipt program cout cin"
- "C++ multiply quantity price"
- "C++ format money output"

<!-- Slide 102 -->

---

## Coding Exercise 2-1: Questions for Reflection

- Which part of the program communicates with the user before calculation?
- Which part communicates the result after calculation?

<!-- Slide 103 -->
